**[0:02](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2)** — - Good evening, everyone. Before I officially call our meeting to order, on behalf of the board, I want to take a few minutes to ask that we remember two of our ICCSD students that we recently lost. Wyatt Irvin, who is a preschooler at Grant Elementary, and Azari Lloyd, who is a third grader at Kirkwood. Both of their passing leaves a void not only in their family lives but also in their schools and the greater ICCSD communities. Wyatt was affectionately called Pie, Wy-Guy, and Wy-Wy by those that cherished him. His loved ones described him as having a contagious laugh

**[0:48](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=48)** — and a huge heart. Wyatt will be lovingly remembered as a funny, affectionate kid who quickly connected with adults in his life and who loved being a big brother. His family shared that he loved reading, writing, counting, playing with cards, swimming, splashing, and exploring all things related to dinosaurs. He also greatly enjoyed his days at the preschool at Christine Grant Elementary where he will be dearly missed by teachers, staff, and his many friends. Our board extends our deepest and heartfelt sympathies to Wyatt's parents, Dylan Irvin and Madeleine Drake-Metzger,

**[1:33](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=93)** — his mother, Trinity Graves, his brother, Everett Irving, and his extended family as they hold tight to their loving memories of Wyatt. Azari Lloyd will be remembered by his loved ones as a child who brought light, laughter, and love to the world. His family and loved ones will hold tight to the memories of him enjoying school, cherishing his friends, dancing, and listening to all kinds of music. Azari has been described as having an infectious laugh, bright smile, and impressive dance moves that had a special way of turning even the darkest days into a rainbow of delight.

**[2:22](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=142)** — - Those who loved Azari will hold tight to the memories of his strength, joy, and loving spirit that will forever remain in their hearts. Our board extends our deepest and heartfelt sympathies to Azari's parents, Angine Cox, his father, Marzell Lloyd, and his siblings, Atarus, Amirra, Azi, and Zae, as well as his many other family members and friends who will continue to honor Azari's memory. - At this time, we ask that you join us in a moment of silence for both Wyatt and Azari.

Students don't just practice notes in this group, they experiment with sounds. We build patterns, which you'll see some patterns we built tonight, and make musical choices together. So we start off with a main idea, and then they take it, and we put it into form, and it's their ideas

**[7:05](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=425)** — and it's their creations that we create. First were the policy series on students. **[33:51](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2031)** — or the desire to continue work on the personal device policy so that we can delineate between elementary and secondary. - Great. - And so that will continue. - Great. Thank you both. Next up we will move on to our presentations tonight, which will be the Unified Sports update by the director of district activities, Director May. I don't know if Superintendent Degner wants to say a few words. - You bet. I'll let her get to the podium here. But we're looking forward to Tunisia tonight talking to you guys about Unified Sports. You may remember we've had some advocacy over the years about Special Olympics and a program the district used to have

**[34:37](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2077)** — and then for a period of time has been on a hiatus. And Tunisia's done a great job of connecting with some stakeholders that are very passionate about this issue in the district. I'm sure some of you guys have had some engagement from those folks and feel like we've put together a good plan here on how to move forward and try to create more opportunities for our students. So with that I'll let her go ahead and share the presentation. - Thank you. I'm really excited about this. So as Matt just mentioned, that we got some people together to kind of look through this because to be honest with you, when someone mentioned Unified Sports, I had no idea what they were talking about. So I went online and did a quick tutorial, I guess you wanna say, to figure it out.

**[35:24](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2124)** — But I do wanna give a shout out to Tom Braverman who did an excellent job of coming to our committee meetings and just filling us all in and really giving us the history of how it started in Iowa City and then what happened to it, kind of how it just kind of disbanded a little bit and how excited he was for us to bring it back. So I'm super excited as well. All right, so I think you guys kind of know a little bit about it, but it's really taking our students with intellectual disabilities and putting them with our students without intellectual disabilities, and really what they're doing is competing together. I know sometimes people think this happens at games when they'll like have a person with a disability and they'll be playing in the game and they'll give 'em a shot

**[36:10](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2170)** — or they'll do like a halftime show. And that's not what Unified Sports is. It's an actual competition where students are competing at their highest level that they can, where they're getting coaching, they're getting practice times, they're actually traveling, and they would be able to be in leagues and that type. So it's a little bit of a different look. It's really giving those students a chance to be a competitor as well with the other students. So our program that we'd like to do is what we're gonna start out pretty slow and then we're gonna really work into it because I really wanted a solid foundation for everyone, and I didn't want 'em thinking they had to do everything in like two seconds. So I wanted to make sure we really went slow

**[36:55](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2215)** — and steady with everything. Everyone that we have talked to and worked with has taken courses to learn about Unified Sports, to learn about the rules of Unified Sports. And our ultimate goal is to become a champion Unified Sports program, which I'll explain that here in a minute. So once the committee got together, we created job descriptions for our building coordinators, and what they basically are is our liaisons. They're there to help the coaches that we're going to hire for each sport, have that connection with teachers, staff members, parents, sometimes they might even have to connect with coaches, ADs just so we have a smooth transition with that program. So at City High we have Christi Johnson.

**[37:41](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2261)** — At Liberty we have Peggy McGuire. And at West we have Tony Rex. And actually we have our committee meeting tomorrow with all three, and we're gonna see what their first activity is that they wanna start with. So that's gonna be exciting to find out what they wanna start with. So what are we gonna do next? I have this whole plan in my head, but then of course it's like I should slow down 'cause they're wonderful people and they really have like amazing ideas, but I do want them to have, you know, a good solid foundation 'cause I want this to continue. So each school's gonna determine their own first activity, what's best for their community, just to get the ball rolling, to get kids excited, to get parents involved as well.

**[38:27](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2307)** — After that's done, we wanna make sure they have some type of activity or competition with that. Then we'll move into a third trimester where I would like them to then start working towards a track and field program. And then we'll have a competition between the three schools with some of the track and field events. And it just depends of course on what the students gravitate towards and what they're good at doing. So that is what's gonna happen. As we do these things, we're gonna work towards what is called a Unified Champion School, and that's where you have to meet 10 national standards by the Special Olympics. And when you meet those 10 standards, you get that distinction of being a banner champion school.

**[39:13](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2353)** — And then what that does is open up more doors for our students where we can start competing, not only across the state but of course across the nation. So I think that's a really lofty goal, but I think we can get to it. Just even starting our programs at each school, we have met four of the 10 standards just by getting it started, having people there in each building, and having a designated coach for each school. So I'm super excited about that. So that's it for the presentation. So if you guys have any questions or anything like that. - Well, as I mentioned to you before the meeting, I am former special ed teacher. I just couldn't love this more. And Superintendent Degner and I had talked about this probably a time or two earlier,

**[40:00](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2400)** — and I'm just very excited, and I appreciate greatly your work, and I like the idea that you want a strong foundation so it keeps going. But thank you. I think this will be a great opportunity for our students.

**[40:16](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2416)** — - Thank you for your presentation, Tunisia. I echo VP Abraham's sentiments. Thank you for bringing this back to life. This is really truly wonderful. - Absolutely. I love it. - And I would agree with that very much, Tunisia. This sounds like a great opportunity for students in the district. Just some questions popped into my mind as I was reading the outline. One, will it be possible for students to meet their physical education requirements, course requirements by participating in these activities? - Absolutely. Our goal is to make sure that it's just as equal as any other sporting activity. So yes, they would be able to get some type of a waiver

**[41:01](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2461)** — if they were involved in those activities. - And then, so is the idea that students will decide on their own to be interested in this as a physical activity or for PE activity and participate in that way, so it's not a recruitment situation, I take it?

**[41:25](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2485)** — But a volunteering or both maybe? - It's a little bit of both, right. So in the beginning we want kids to be involved, so we want them to choose that, but we definitely wanna keep the program going and that may require us to go out and recruit, and that's okay too. So I'm just encouraging the building coordinators to do what they do, what's necessary for each different school culture. So it'll depend on the school. - And then finally, will our new practice facilities at the high schools help this program? - Absolutely will help this program. It's gonna open up doors. It'll give more space of course for us to try other activities as we go. Because that's one of the things right now that's gonna be tough is if we decide to do like basketball, it'll be really tough to find that space, right?

**[42:11](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2531)** — So as those facilities start to open up, we'll have way more space to do those things. - I'm very glad to hear that. That's been a considerable investment on our part in terms of facilities, and it's very great to see those facilities being available and supporting all students. - Absolutely. - Thank you, Director May, for getting this program up and running again. And also please extend our thanks to your committee members, the building folks that will also work with you. This is a program that, you know, I've heard over the years from parents with children with intellectual disabilities who can't necessarily participate

**[42:57](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2577)** — in our regular activities and sporting events. And it's also something that's near and dear to my heart, having a sibling that was also left out of these type of activities. So I am really excited to see it taking off in our district. Please keep us informed of the activities that's planned and organized. I'm sure a lot of us would love to come and cheer our new athletes on. So thank you again. - Thank you. - Next up we'll move on to our action items for tonight. First up is the interfund loan. And I don't know if Deputy Superintendent Ramey will kick us off.

**[43:42](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2622)** — - Good evening. Thank you, President Malone. As the board can see, we didn't have a presentation this evening on this resolution as it relates to the loan that was made between two accounts last fall. We do recognize that this raises some questions about overall kind of our quarterly financial and what does this mean for us from a financial standpoint. We are currently working on putting together materials to come back and talk with the board in February. We don't see this as a one and done this evening. And so while we don't have a presentation, we do wanna start there by letting the board know that there will be additional material coming from us in terms for discussion with the board at our meeting coming up in a couple of weeks. However, with that said, we will entertain any questions the board has prior to the board voting on this resolution.

**[44:32](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2672)** — - [Malone] Directors, feel free.

**[44:37](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2677)** — - I can go ahead. Thank you. So thank you...

**[44:45](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2685)** — Deputy Superintendent- - Chase is fine. - I just wanna call you Chase. (everyone laughs) All right, so yeah, thank you for that opening. This does, you know, bring up several financial and governance concerns. So, you know, we on the board, and the public may not be aware of some of these school finance issues, but on the board we do understand that cash flow can sometimes be an issue. We don't always have cash when it comes time to pay our bills, even though eventually it will all even out in the end. And so occasionally districts do need to make these loans or interfund transfers. It can be a matter of timing. But I think what makes this particular loan a bit irregular

**[45:31](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2731)** — in my opinion is number one, the size of it, $10 million, and, you know, so the large amount and then also the fact that there was no authorization upfront. And five months later we are giving the authorization. So, you know, in the interest of making sure that the public is well informed about this and also to ensure that we are putting processes in place to sort of make sure that this type of thing doesn't happen again, I sent a series of questions over the weekend by email to the team, and I thought it would be a good idea if we just ran through those questions and answers tonight on the board just for the sake of education for our community.

**[46:18](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2778)** — And I'm sure you have a copy of those. Okay. So if you don't mind, I'll just start with the first question, which is the history of interfund transfers, has our district needed to make other interfund transfers in recent years due to cash flow issues? If yes, provide the dates and funds. And if not, please explain what circumstances make this year different from prior years. So, okay. - According to our records, the last interfund loan that was approved by the board of directors was in September of 2017. And it was actually a transfer from SAVE to the bond proceeds to cover the cash flow issues related to construction from our One Visions facility master plan. And so that's the last one we show a record

**[47:04](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2824)** — of board approval. And I'll add on, I'm gonna jump to one of your, I'm cheating 'cause I do have your questions. To one of your other questions. I think it relates to something you said, Director Finch, and really relates to this question as well. But one of the reasons maybe the records don't indicate that is because board policy 701.2 actually does not require board approval for what this would be, a loan between one fund to another. And really that also speaks to your question about the timing. And so when it came back and we were asked for a interest rate to pay the fund back, we kind of took a pause and we reached out to legal counsel.

**[47:51](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2871)** — And so legal counsel is the one that suggested that we get the board approval for this. And so at the time when the business office was looking at it, as I said, the policy didn't necessarily require that. But when we went around to legal counsel, they suggested we bring that. And that notification didn't come to the superintendent's office until after winter break. And so that's really why that lag is. As soon as we got that advice, we put this resolution together in time to get it on tonight's board agenda so we could get that approval. To your point earlier about what processes are we putting in place, that's something we've already started to look at that we will take the policy back to legal counsel as well bring it to P&G when the seven hundreds come before P&G in two months, so that we can look at that

**[48:37](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2917)** — to see the enhancements we want to make in terms of those checks and balances as we move forward. - Okay. Okay. Thank you. So my next question was about financial reporting. So... And I was confused because there's nowhere in our quarterly reports or any of our financial reporting where we could see this loan. And so I'm also wondering about what processes or what changes we'll be making to assure that in the future those types of things are visible for the board. - Kurt, do you wanna speak to that one? - Sure, so the quarterly financial report

**[49:24](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=2964)** — doesn't show this specific transaction because it was completed on the banking side, but not properly recorded on the accounting side. So due to that unfortunate mistake, that's why that quarterly financial report doesn't show that transaction. So in the future we'll set up steps to monitor that and better reflect that on those reports. - Okay, and then speaking of that quarterly financial report that came through on September 9th, that board meeting, and so there was, and I apologize, we don't have this up for everyone, but there was a table that was shared during that quarterly financial report where it reported the days, and I'm just, I'm asking 'cause I wanna understand this better. The days net cash ratio suggested a market improvement

**[50:13](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3013)** — from the prior fiscal year of 47.6 FY '24,

**[50:21](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3021)** — which was in the red, to an improvement of 76.4, which was in the yellow. So is that improvement because we took the loan or why does that show that there's an improvement there? - So that improvement would've been noted across all funds, all cash positions. And so that was not a general fund specific metric, whereas this transaction was between the health insurance fund and the general fund. So for instance, our net cash position and SAVE probably went up because we didn't have as many large scale projects going on at that time, and so that would've added to that day's net cash on hand number that you saw there. - Okay, so if our cash position was improved,

**[51:10](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3070)** — I'm just trying to understand why we needed to take the loan. I guess can you sort of reconcile that for me and for the public? - Sure. I mean, our revenues are fund based, and so as the example I just made in terms of the SAVE dollars, we would get that money, and that's not earmarked for the general fund, but that would still be reflected in an overall net cash position increase. So the general fund could be in a poorer position than any other fund, but when they're all taken together, that's what that number would represent. - Okay, that makes sense. I'm just trying to figure out what made this year different that we needed to take a loan whereas last year we didn't need to take the loan.

**[51:59](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3119)** — - I think those are some of the things we're still trying to better finalize and zero in on, that we'd be in a better position to talk in more detail when we come back in February. But from a general sense, we know that in September and October we're not, unlike a lot of districts where cashflow gets tight, as a public school, we get our two largest payments in October and in April when we get our property tax receipts. As we start the fiscal year, September is traditionally one of our largest payrolls as we bring on new employees and we get the school year started. And so it is not uncommon for that to be a point of year

**[52:45](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3165)** — where our cash flow is very tight, Director Finch. But to your question about why this year and not last year, those are some of the things that we are still trying to look into deeper, and we'll have a better opportunity to speak to it when we're back together. - Okay, so next month or- - Yes. But it's really, one of the general is that- - Okay. - One of our largest tax receipt payments, the two that we get comes in October, and so we hit one of our biggest payrolls at the very end of the cycle that we're trying to get to our next large property tax receipt. - Okay. And all that having been said, and I understand and appreciate all of that. I'm still, and I think we're not gonna be able to get that answer,

**[53:32](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3212)** — but I'm still wondering why this wasn't communicated to the board in that September quarterly financial report. You know, I think about some of the decisions that we made since then, some of the, you know... The board needs to have an understanding of our financial situation, and the fact that we had to take the loan does suggest to me that perhaps our cash position, our unspent authorized budget or balance is not as robust as we thought it was. And so I'm just trying to understand why, you know, aside from the fact that it was not authorized, why was it not communicated?

**[54:20](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3260)** — - Absolutely. And we can note that to bring back when we come in February. - Okay. Okay. I just have a few more questions. So the impact to the insurance fund, my question on that end, is there any impact to that, any risk that we should be aware of on the insurance fund?

**[54:47](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3287)** — - At present, no, we do not believe the insurance fund is at risk, and we believe that we can have that loan repaid to the insurance fund in the next six months. That loan, that $10 million was not cash on hand at the time. We did some checking, and we believe that it had been invested in CD since about 2017. And so we feel that at present, we know that money needs to be repaid, but in terms of is the fund at risk, no, we do not believe it's currently at risk. - Okay. And then do you anticipate any future cash flow actions in terms of loans or anticipatory warrants coming down the pike?

**[55:39](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3339)** — - Possibly. We're still working through that. Again, as I mentioned, we get our two largest property tax receipts in October and again in April. And so as you look at payroll in March and April, it's something that we want to be conscious of. And so still looking at our cash position where we are and working through those details as we approach that. So possible, but I can't say definitively one way or another this evening. - Okay. And then question six was in regards to our unspent authorized balance, an updated estimate. You're gonna come back to us next month with that,

**[56:26](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3386)** — with any impact of interest payments, which could be as high as 400,000. You know, how much would the interest payment be on that? I was doing the calculation in my head. - Do you know the...

**[56:45](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3405)** — - [Kurt] I'm not sure. It'd be prudent to even speculate at this point- - Okay. - [Kurt] Based on that. - Okay. Sure. Fair enough. And then question number seven, Chase, you already addressed that, so thank you very much. - Of course. - I would be interested in understanding if any other directors have any questions or concerns about this as well. So thank you.

**[57:13](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3433)** — - President Malone, I have just a few observations and comments, I think. For one thing, to me this was a normal administrative function, which was properly done for the cash flow positions that the district encounters over the course of an entire fiscal year. So I'm not particularly, I'm certainly not alarmed by it, and I'm not looking for any kind of, anything that I think needs correction. I think reporting to the board is a good idea. That's a good idea. I'm not sure why there's an interest payment that we are considering and repaying this transfer of funds.

**[58:01](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3481)** — To me an interest payment simply means that we're gonna take money from our operating funds and transfer it to our permanent or our ongoing asset funds.

**[58:18](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3498)** — So I'm not sure what... I think the interest payment is hurting our overall operation. And I don't see its purpose. But that's something you can address, and it may be a legal requirement that I should just be quiet about.

**[58:35](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3515)** — - You don't need to be quiet about anything, Director Eastham, but it is probably one of those things that we don't have the power to change. And yes, it's something that we were informed that we need to put in place when you're talking about taking money from a fund that's set up for one purpose and then loaning it to a fund for a different purpose. When we do that, we were told that the requirement is that we have to pay interest on that. - Yeah, for a temporary situation. - Correct. - And the money will repaid to the originating fund or source fund, okay. But anyway, I'm not arguing that with you. The other thing is, as I understand it, the budget for the current fiscal year has not been changed.

**[59:27](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3567)** — - No, it has not. - We have not changed the revenue projections and we have not changed the expense projections for the current year physical budget. Go ahead. - I think that's... It connects back to Chase's earlier answer, Charlie, when Director Finch was asking the questions about impact to the unspent budget. I think some of our nervousness about the March one would be as we're trying to track those expenses throughout the year. So that's something the team has been digging into the last week, is to have a better reflection of what are those month over month expenses to not only diagnosis what we thought was a simple timing issue and a procedural step that was missed to understanding if there's also a long-term expense issue that we need to monitor and correct.

**[1:00:15](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3615)** — From a revenue perspective, it's more on the timing and the receipt of those payments and when they come in and the challenges created on that. I think what we're trying to do a careful analysis of now are the overall expenses that are going out as well. - Okay, well to me reviewing our expenses over the course of fiscal year and comparing what we're actually experiencing and what we projected is a good thing to do. And the answers that we get as a result of that review

**[1:00:48](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3648)** — will enable us to make good decisions about taking any corrective actions during the course of a fiscal year that affects our budget for that year. So what I'm trying to...

**[1:01:04](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3664)** — I think project here is I think, I'm not dismayed by this situation. As I said, I think it was a good administrative decision. I have no idea how on earth as a board member I could have voted against this fund's transfer for this purpose. Since doing that, as I understand it, would've resulted in our inability to make our obligations and day to day in terms of cash obligations, cash payments that are required. And if some of those cash payments were going to be for payroll, I would be trying to vote for not meeting our payroll obligations when there was money sitting in another account, which is the district's account, district's funds that is available to meet those payroll obligations

**[1:01:53](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3713)** — in a more or less temporary situation. And then reestablishing our full assets when we received our revenue later in the year.

**[1:02:12](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3732)** — So if I'm misrepresenting what you've reported to us, please let me know. Otherwise, I'm looking forward, you know,

**[1:02:29](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3749)** — to just reviewing. - Sure. - I'm particularly interested in why the state is so klutzy in making revenue payments to us. - Sure. - That puts districts in these situations. - I don't think that you misrepresent anything. And I appreciate those comments, Director Eastham, and I also appreciate the comments about communication and what Director Finch offered about process. I mean, we're a learning organization, and we never want the board to be caught off guard or surprised. And so we always wanna look at our processes and how we can enhance those and make sure that we're being good stewards of our finances, that we're representing what we're doing to the community, and that we're putting the board in a good place

**[1:03:16](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3796)** — to have those conversations as well. And so I think this is a great conversation to have. We always need those opportunities to enhance the processes that we use. And so we don't see this as negative. Like I said, we're a learning organization, not only on the teaching and learning side, but also on the operations side. And so as I mentioned in the answer to one of the questions to Director Finch, we believe that we have a path to pay this forward. We know that we need to have some additional conversation about our cash flow, about what the impact is on our UAB, and potentially how we do our quarterly reports. And so those are all things that we're working on. It's just we felt the need tonight was to put this resolution in front of you to open up that communication

**[1:04:01](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3841)** — and then we come back to those other pieces at a meeting next month. - All right. The last thing I just wanna mention is that during our budget discussions, I recall hearing several conversations about cash flow issues, potential cash flow issues over the course of a fiscal year that were part of those discussions from time to time.

**[1:04:36](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3876)** — - I'll chime in. Deputy Superintendent Ramey, you said that there isn't a requirement for the resolution, is that what you said earlier? - That's my... Well, based on our guidance from legal counsel, there is. Based on our current board policy, it does not require board approval. So I think that's one of the things we need to review and square. - What it does say in board policy is that temporary transfer loans of funds permitted between funds must be repaid to the originating funds with interest by October 1st, the following end of the fiscal year. Under that it says it is the responsibility of the board secretary, not thrown on you Secretary Colvin, but the board secretary to make recommendations

**[1:05:21](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3921)** — to the board regarding transfers and to provide the documentation justifying the transfer. So it may not require resolution, but if transfers are going to happen, we are supposed to know. And if we are a policy forward board, then we need to make sure that our policy is still being followed. My main question is the...

**[1:05:50](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=3950)** — First off, this is costing $394,000 to our general fund. So that is, from my understanding, whatever's left over in the general fund, we get to roll over in the unspent cash balance, correct? - [Kurt] Yes. - Okay. So that's money that we could be rolling over. So that's my first concern. Then after last budget reduction, the board established a UAB of five to 10%. We still have not hit that number yet. Is the cash flow problem a result of not hitting the unspent cash balance that IASB tells us that we should be hitting, that we have as a board past policy recommending, is that part of the problem?

**[1:06:42](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4002)** — - I don't see them jumping in. - Well, I'm trying to think through it. - I see those two separate issues. And so our unspent balance and what you're talking about there is really the separate concept from cash, right? And the cash flow is really about receipts in and expenditures out. And so again, back to, you know, the original timing issue that created this one with the payments at those two times, to answer Charlie's question, that's when the state redistributes property tax payments. And so if people don't pay their property taxes, we don't get that money. If there's property tax reform, that could mess up how much we get at those two times of the year. So no, they're connected, but they're not for necessarily a question you're asking in a sense. If you spend over the amount of cash that you have, then yes, that can negatively impact your unspent balance.

**[1:07:29](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4049)** — But it's not really the way you asked it, if that makes sense. - But how do we get to a point where we don't have the cash on hand? 'Cause 2017 was last time that we had to do an interfund transfer. I was told today by someone else that they did an interfund transfer like this back in 2013 or '14. So it's been over a decade. How do we go a decade without an issue like this but have one this year? Is it because we're at 1.9%, just under 2% unspent cash balance last year transitioning into 3.5% this year? Or what is... How do we go 10 years without... How do we go 10 years meeting payroll

**[1:08:16](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4096)** — and then suddenly we're not able to meet payroll? - Yeah, I- - I'm not trying to be mean, because I mean- - No, I think your question, I'm not resisting it. I think your question's fair. I think it goes back to the follow up conversation we'd like to have with you on the 10th, right? I mean, I think the original working knowledge that we had about this was it was a timing issue. And then as this has continued on and as we've dug into it, I think that's what we'd wanna come back to you and talk about is what is the reason for the problematic nature of those expenses coming out. And then having, you know, potentially another concern here when we come to March about, again, the timing of the payment, making sure we have enough cash to go out. I think Chase did a nice job, explained before that it's always tight at certain times of the year.

**[1:09:02](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4142)** — The fact to where Director Finch started about the size and scope of this transfer, I think those are questions we've been working to unpack here in the last two weeks as well that are fair and ones that, you know, again, we deserve to provide answers to. I can't speak to time before ours here. I know this is the first cash transfer we've asked for in that regard. And even the last one we documented to you tonight was really in regards to facility proceeds. And so, again, I know we'll want to come back and provide you more of that information. I think we can give you a better look this time rather than just talking to generalities or platitudes. - Got it. And to me, because it is payroll, this is making sure people have the money

**[1:09:48](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4188)** — to pay their bills, to pay for their daycare, to pay for their meals, to pay for their gas, their mortgage, whatever it may be. We got lucky that we have this other fund that we can take from, but I mean, it's not something that should happen with an organization like this, especially an organization that means so much to so many people in the community. And I've heard about it already from multiple people and they're like, "Why? What's going on here?" And I don't know if it is just that we're playing this dance so close

**[1:10:33](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4233)** — to that $0 in our spending or what it is. I mean, I have to acknowledge that there's always a difficulty in balancing costs, and we have, I mean, people want 1,000 different pathways throughout their secondary schools, and we have people that want pay raises and not having to pay for insurance costs. Everybody wants small neighborhood elementary schools. Some of us don't get them. Others want buildings that are new and fancy and shiny that cost somewhere between 225 to $600,000 to provide operational costs. I mean, and we don't even, that would be the athletic field houses, and we don't know what the cost is of black box theater

**[1:11:19](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4279)** — at Liberty or the new wing at City. While everybody wants small and stable class sizes for their children. And I mean, we can blame it on the state as much as we want to, but that doesn't change the situation that we have to deal with and that is trying to work within this vacuum of all these wants, right? And I mean, it's just, but if we're going to kind of play the shell game of balancing money and moving it here and there to make sure that things get covered when they need to get covered, we just need to be honest and transparent to the community instead of making them think things are all right. And I mean, that is on me as a board member

**[1:12:05](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4325)** — as much as it is on everybody else around this table and everybody that has a part in it. But part of it is that, make no mistake, this is gonna take $400,000 from our general fund that could have been a 10% increase in our general fund. And that is... It is what it is. But I have to acknowledge that this is part of the game that we're playing when we're that close. Thank you. - So can I just, I wanna ask you what you're trying to get at there, and correct me if I'm wrong, but I think what you're we're trying to say is that if we had some sky high unspent authorized balance, which I know some districts do,

**[1:12:51](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4371)** — which is why we run into some issues with the SSA every year, right? Because some districts do have this big stockpile of unspent authorized balance. But if we had let's say $40 million in our UAB, we wouldn't be borrowing, and then we wouldn't be incurring interest. I think that's what you're trying, I'm not suggesting that we should have a UAB that large, but I think that's what you're trying to say, correct? - Yes. Thank you. - Okay.

**[1:13:22](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4402)** — - My question related to this, I know back in November at the November 11th meeting, we approved exhibits that was presented then to the School Budget Review Committee. Does this loan get reported as part of that or should it been or do you have to go back and share that? It has nothing... - No, that was part of our original question was is this a loan that needs to be reported to the SBRC? Is that present loan? And that's where we got the guidance from legal that this needed to be approved by the board as a resolution, but that no, this did not require any sort of report

**[1:14:08](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4448)** — to the DE or to the SBRC. - And if it continues, like we continually have to do such loans for a period of time, it still doesn't have to get reported out except to the board? - Well, I mean, it's not our plan to do it on an ongoing basis, and so we didn't necessarily ask that- - Okay. - Ask that question. - Okay. Any other questions or comments from directors? Director Eastham? - It just occurs to me that at the end of the fiscal year we will have, I'm confident we will... We will have repaid this loan, this interfund transfer, and with the required interest,

**[1:14:55](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4495)** — and this conversation is pretty much muted at that point. - Potentially not by the... Well, six months, so... - Well at some point. Some near... According to the terms of the law. - Yes. Sorry. Yes. Yes. - So we'll have met the terms of our borrowing? - Correct. - Okay. - Are there any additional questions or discussions from directors? Is there a motion to approve the resolution? - Move approval of the resolution. - Is there a second? - Second. - All right.

**[1:15:40](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4540)** — Any additional discussion? Hearing none. Board Secretary Colvin, ready to vote. - Online voting is open.

**[1:16:11](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4571)** — - Well, Kim, I don't see a voting box popping up on my system here, but I will vote yes for them. - Thank you, Director Eastham. Director Abraham, I don't have yours. Oh, I got it. All votes have been cast, and the motion carries with all directors voting in favor. - Thank you. Next action item is the Forevergreen Road expansion phase one for Van Allen Elementary School temporary construction easement. I don't know if any of our directors, administration wanted to make any comments or if any board directors had any questions or comments. Hearing none. Is there a motion to approve? - I move. - Motion to approve. Or second. - Okay. So then Horn-Frasier and Director Lingo.

**[1:18:51](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4731)** — for us to pull together that day. And looking at the other Fridays, there wasn't room to schedule a new one. Of course the largest one is property tax reform that's being discussed in Des Moines and where they'll land on that. I do believe they'll probably accomplish something with property tax reform before the end of the legislative session. An impact point for us that I know there's a lot of heavy advocacy going on around right now is in terms of SAVE dollars that a couple of the proposals have included redistributing the SAVE proceeds away from school districts and to help with the backfill for property tax reform.

**[1:19:38](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4778)** — There's 175 districts in the state that are already bonded against SAVE revenues. We're one of those districts that would be looking to do that in the future, of course, to fund our F&P 2.3. And so that is something we'll wanna monitor closely. Without the ability to do that, that would obviously change our plans immensely for what we could accomplish there. We've heard encouraging news from our local legislators about their skepticism that they'll actually be able to use SAVE differently that way or that they'll be able to redistribute that from schools and then as well as some of our folks that represent our professional organization. So we'll remain hopeful there, but I think we need to continue to share the impact point of that and recognize how many school districts that would impact, not just us here in Iowa City. There's also some pre-filed bills with TAG,

**[1:20:27](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4827)** — talented and gifted, in our advanced pathways from the DE. In that regard, you have some impact points on a full unit of government physical educational requirements K-5. There's a student discipline bill or a couple student discipline bills that have been talked about at the State House. Gender theory and sexual orientation restriction for grade 7-12 has also been proposed. And so there's a few classroom impacts of course being talked about as well in terms of education policy. We don't believe we'll receive an SSA number anytime soon knowing that that's probably gonna be somewhat dependent on where they land with property tax reform. SSA seems to be getting some very different viewpoints.

**[1:21:15](https://youtu.be/E5g1GLUKtUI?t=4875)** — House Republicans seem to be closer to zero, or excuse me, seem to be higher than their Senate colleagues. The House Republicans are talking sometimes even three and four. Senate Republicans are more talking zero. The governor's been in the middle at two. So, you know, if you have a glass ball or a magic eight ball and could predict that for us, we'd love that. But as we're in the budget planning cycle, of course that's a huge variable for us as we try to do that budget planning. So that's the legislative update I provide. We'll continue to do those every time we meet and let you know the things that we're paying attention to and keep it on our radar.
